title.th: ช่องว่างข้อมูลแพทย์แผนไทย

hook.en: Thailand has over 30,000 traditional medicine practitioners, but treatment data is rarely recorded or connected to modern health systems.
hook.th: ประเทศไทยมีผู้ประกอบวิชาชีพแพทย์แผนไทยกว่า 30,000 คน แต่ข้อมูลการรักษาแทบไม่ถูกบันทึกหรือเชื่อมต่อกับระบบสุขภาพสมัยใหม่

challenge.en: How might we create a bridge that digitizes traditional medicine treatment outcomes and makes them interoperable with modern health records — enabling integrated, evidence-based care?
challenge.th: เราจะสร้างระบบที่แปลงข้อมูลการรักษาแพทย์แผนไทยให้เป็นดิจิทัล และเชื่อมต่อกับบันทึกสุขภาพสมัยใหม่ได้ เพื่อนำไปสู่การดูแลสุขภาพแบบบูรณาการที่มีหลักฐานรองรับได้อย่างไร?

severity.justification: Millions of patients receive potentially uncoordinated care; herb-drug interactions remain undocumented; treatment outcomes cannot be aggregated for evidence-based practice.

difficulty.justification: Requires developing new terminology standards, building consensus across diverse traditional medicine systems, significant investment in rural IT infrastructure.

impact.justification: Successful solution would enable evidence-based integration of traditional medicine affecting 80% of world population; reduce adverse events; preserve indigenous knowledge.

urgency.justification: Aging populations increasing multi-modal treatment use; traditional knowledge being lost as elder practitioners pass away; WHO pushing for traditional medicine integration.

statistics.stat: Thailand has over 30,000 traditional medicine practitioners registered with DTAM
statistics.source: WHO Global Report on Traditional Medicine 2025

statistics.stat: Over 80% of global population uses traditional medicine, particularly in Asia, Africa, and Latin America
statistics.source: WHO Global Report on Traditional Medicine 2025

statistics.stat: Thai traditional medicine has been integrated into Universal Health Coverage since 2002
Only 15 OECD countries have nationally implemented electronic health record systems with adequate interoperability
8 of 10 ASEAN Member States have integrated traditional medicine into public health service systems
statistics.source: ASEAN Joint Report on Primary Health Care 2023

painPoints: Heavy reliance on traditional medicine as primary healthcare
High utilization of both traditional and modern medicine
Increased risk of adverse drug reactions
Lack of standardized digital documentation tools

primary.role: Department of Thai Traditional and Alternative Medicine (DTAM)

rootCauses.cause: Epistemological differences between traditional and modern medicine
rootCauses.explanation: Traditional medicine follows holistic, person-centered approaches with different diagnostic frameworks that do not map directly to ICD codes.

rootCauses.cause: Lack of standardized terminology and coding systems
rootCauses.explanation: Unlike modern medicine with standardized codes (ICD-10/11, SNOMED CT), traditional medicine lacks universally accepted diagnostic and treatment codes.
Southeast Asian countries operate health systems in silos without standardized data formats; even Thailand's 1,300+ hospitals use varying IT systems.

rootCauses.cause: Insufficient investment in traditional medicine digital infrastructure
rootCauses.explanation: Traditional medicine facilities often lack computers, internet connectivity, and trained IT personnel.
Traditional medicine knowledge has been transmitted orally across generations with practitioners often maintaining secrecy about formulations.

existingSolutions.name: HDC-TTM Service
existingSolutions.approach: Real-time monitoring system launched 2013 by DTAM, NHSO, and MOPH ICT Center; collects service records from facilities nationwide
strengths: Integrates with MOPH Health Data Center
weaknesses: Focuses on service volume, not clinical outcomes
Limited interoperability with modern medicine records

existingSolutions.name: EC Connect (Hong Kong eHealth)
existingSolutions.approach: Clinical management system for Chinese medicine practitioners that enables access to both Chinese and Western medicine records
strengths: True integration of Chinese and Western medicine records
weaknesses: Specific to TCM, not Thai Traditional Medicine

existingSolutions.name: MyPostnatalSys (Malaysia)
existingSolutions.approach: Electronic health record prototype for integrating Traditional Malay Medicine and Modern Medicine using HL7 FHIR standards
strengths: Specifically designed for postnatal care integration
weaknesses: Limited to postnatal care use case

opportunityAreas.description: Develop HL7 FHIR resources and profiles specifically designed for traditional medicine diagnoses, treatments, and outcomes
opportunityAreas.potentialImpact: Enable semantic interoperability between traditional and modern medicine systems globally

opportunityAreas.description: Develop simple, voice-enabled mobile apps that allow traditional medicine practitioners to document consultations using natural language
opportunityAreas.potentialImpact: Overcome digital literacy barriers; capture treatment data at point of care

opportunityAreas.area: Integration of HDC-TTM with Hospital EMRs
opportunityAreas.description: Upgrade existing HDC-TTM Service to connect with hospital electronic medical records, enabling bidirectional data exchange
opportunityAreas.potentialImpact: Leverage existing infrastructure; enable integrated care for patients receiving both types of treatment

resources.title: WHO Global Traditional Medicine Strategy 2025-2034
resources.description: Strategic framework for integrating safe and effective traditional medicine into national health systems

resources.title: HITAP Case Study: Real-Time Monitoring System for Thai Traditional Medicine
resources.description: Detailed analysis of Thailand's HDC-TTM implementation and lessons for other countries

resources.title: ASEAN Joint Report on Primary Health Care 2023
resources.description: Comprehensive analysis of PHC capacities including traditional medicine integration across ASEAN

keyInsights: Thailand is uniquely positioned for traditional medicine digitization with DTAM infrastructure, UHC integration since 2002, and HDC-TTM monitoring system - but the data remains siloed from modern medicine records.
The key barrier is not technical but semantic: traditional medicine concepts do not have standard mappings to modern medical terminologies like ICD-10/11 or SNOMED CT.
WHO's recent inclusion of Ayurveda, Siddha, and Unani codes in international health intervention coding creates a precedent for traditional medicine standardization.
Mobile-first solutions are essential given the rural distribution of traditional medicine practitioners and high smartphone penetration (76.58% in Thailand).

tangibleEquivalent.en: Like burning a library of 30,000 medical books every generation.
tangibleEquivalent.th: เหมือนการเผาห้องสมุดตำราแพทย์ 30,000 เล่มทิ้งในทุกๆ รุ่น
